title: Chain of Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models

paragraphs.text:
## **Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models**

**Jason Wei Xuezhi Wang Dale Schuurmans Maarten Bosma Brian Ichter Fei Xia Ed H. Chi Quoc V. Le Denny Zhou**

Google Research, Brain Team `{jasonwei,dennyzhou}@google.com`

## **Abstract**

We explore how generating a _chain of thought_ —a series of intermediate reasoning steps—significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models via a simple method called _chain-ofthought prompting_ , where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting.

Experiments on three large language models show that chain-of-thought prompting improves performance on a range of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. The empirical gains can be striking. For instance, prompting a PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even

## **1 Introduction**

The NLP landscape has recently been revolutionized by language models (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ). Scaling up the size of language models has been shown to confer a range of benefits, such as improved performance and sample efficiency (Kaplan et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020, _inter alia_ ). However, scaling up model size alone has not proved sufficient for achieving high performance on challenging tasks such as arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning (Rae et al., 2021).

This work explores how the reasoning ability of large language models can be unlocked by a simple method motivated by two ideas. First, techniques for arithmetic reasoning can benefit from generating natural language rationales that lead to the final answer. Prior work has given models the ability to generate natural language intermediate steps by training from scratch (Ling et al., 2017) or finetuning a pretrained model (Cobbe et al., 2021), in addition to neuro-symbolic methods that use formal languages instead of natural language (Roy and Roth, 2015; Chiang and Chen, 2019; Amini et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019). Second, large language models offer the exciting

prospect of in-context few-shot learning via _prompting_ . That is, instead of finetuning a separate language model checkpoint for each new task, one can simply “prompt” the model with a few input–output exemplars demonstrating the task. Remarkably, this has been successful for a range of simple question-answering tasks (Brown et al., 2020).

Both of the above ideas, however, have key limitations. For rationale-augmented training and finetuning methods, it is costly to create a large set of high quality rationales, which is much more complicated than simple input–output pairs used in normal machine learning. For the traditional fewshot prompting method used in Brown et al. (2020), it works poorly on tasks that require reasoning abilities, and often does not improve substantially with increasing language model scale (Rae et al., 2021). In this paper, we combine the strengths of these two ideas in a way that avoids their limitations. Specifically, we explore the ability of language models to perform few-shot prompting for reasoning tasks, given a prompt that consists of triples: _⟨_ input, _chain of thought_ , output _⟩_ . A _chain of thought_ is a series of intermediate natural language reasoning steps that lead to the final output, and we refer to this approach as _chain-of-thought prompting_ . An example prompt is shown in Figure 1.

We present empirical evaluations on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks, showing that chain-of-thought prompting outperforms standard prompting, sometimes to a striking degree. Figure 2 illustrates one such result—on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems (Cobbe et al., 2021), chain-of-thought prompting with PaLM 540B outperforms standard prompting by a large margin and achieves new state-of-the-art performance. A prompting only approach is important because it does not require a large training dataset and because a single model checkpoint can perform many tasks without loss of generality. This work underscores how large language models can learn via a few examples with natural language data about the task (c.f. automatically learning the patterns underlying inputs and outputs via a large training dataset).

## **2 Chain-of-Thought Prompting**

Consider one’s own thought process when solving a complicated reasoning task such as a multi-step math word problem. It is typical to decompose the problem into intermediate steps and solve each before giving the final answer: _“After Jane gives 2 flowers to her mom she has 10 . . . then after she gives 3 to her dad she will have 7 . . . so the answer is 7.”_ The goal of this paper is to endow language models with the ability to generate a similar _chain of thought_ —a coherent series of intermediate reasoning steps that lead to the final answer for a problem. We will show that sufficiently large

2

language models can generate chains of thought if demonstrations of chain-of-thought reasoning are provided in the exemplars for few-shot prompting.

Figure 1 shows an example of a model producing a chain of thought to solve a math word problem that it would have otherwise gotten incorrect. The chain of thought in this case resembles a solution and can interpreted as one, but we still opt to call it a chain of thought to better capture the idea that it mimics a step-by-step thought process for arriving at the answer (and also, solutions/explanations typically come _after_ the final answer (Narang et al., 2020; Wiegreffe et al., 2022; Lampinen et al., 2022, _inter alia_ )).
Chain-of-thought prompting has several attractive properties as an approach for facilitating reasoning in language models.

1. First, chain of thought, in principle, allows models to decompose multi-step problems into intermediate steps, which means that additional computation can be allocated to problems that require more reasoning steps.

2. Second, a chain of thought provides an interpretable window into the behavior of the model, suggesting how it might have arrived at a particular answer and providing opportunities to debug where the reasoning path went wrong (although fully characterizing a model’s computations that support an answer remains an open question).

3. Third, chain-of-thought reasoning can be used for tasks such as math word problems, commonsense reasoning, and symbolic manipulation, and is potentially applicable (at least in principle) to any task that humans can solve via language.

4. Finally, chain-of-thought reasoning can be readily elicited in sufficiently large off-the-shelf language models simply by including examples of chain of thought sequences into the exemplars of few-shot prompting.
In empirical experiments, we will observe the utility of chain-of-thought prompting for arithmetic reasoning (Section 3), commonsense reasoning (Section 4), and symbolic reasoning (Section 5).

## **3 Arithmetic Reasoning**

We begin by considering math word problems of the form in Figure 1, which measure the arithmetic reasoning ability of language models. Though simple for humans, arithmetic reasoning is a task where language models often struggle (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Patel et al., 2021, _inter alia_ ). Strikingly, chainof-thought prompting when used with the 540B parameter language model performs comparably with task-specific finetuned models on several tasks, even achieving new state of the art on the challenging GSM8K benchmark (Cobbe et al., 2021).

## **3.1 Experimental Setup**

We explore chain-of-thought prompting for various language models on multiple benchmarks.

**Benchmarks.** We consider the following five math word problem benchmarks: **(1)** the **GSM8K** benchmark of math word problems (Cobbe et al., 2021), **(2)** the **SVAMP** dataset of math word problems with varying structures (Patel et al., 2021), **(3)** the **ASDiv** dataset of diverse math word problems (Miao et al., 2020), **(4)** the **AQuA** dataset of algebraic word problems, and **(5)** the **MAWPS** benchmark (Koncel-Kedziorski et al., 2016). Example problems are given in Appendix Table 12.

**Standard prompting.** For the baseline, we consider standard few-shot prompting, popularized by Brown et al. (2020), in which a language model is given in-context exemplars of input–output pairs before outputting a prediction for a test-time example. Exemplars are formatted as questions and answers. The model gives the answer directly, as shown in Figure 1 (left).

**Chain-of-thought prompting.** Our proposed approach is to augment each exemplar in few-shot prompting with a chain of thought for an associated answer, as illustrated in Figure 1 (right). As most of the datasets only have an evaluation split, we manually composed a set of eight few-shot exemplars with chains of thought for prompting—Figure 1 (right) shows one chain of thought exemplar, and the full set of exemplars is given in Appendix Table 20. (These particular exemplars did not undergo prompt engineering; robustness is studied in Section 3.4 and Appendix A.2.) To investigate whether chain-of-thought prompting in this form can successfully elicit successful reasoning across a range of

**Language models.** We evaluate five large language models. The first is **GPT-3** (Brown et al., 2020), for which we use text-ada-001, text-babbage-001, text-curie-001, and text-davinci-002, which presumably correspond to InstructGPT models of 350M, 1.3B, 6.7B, and 175B parameters (Ouyang et al., 2022).The second is **LaMDA** (Thoppilan et al., 2022), which has models of 422M, 2B, 8B, 68B, and 137B parameters. The third is **PaLM** , which has models of 8B, 62B, and 540B parameters. The fourth is **UL2 20B** (Tay et al., 2022), and the fifth is **Codex** (Chen et al., 2021, code-davinci-002 in the OpenAI API). We sample from the models via greedy decoding (though follow-up work shows chain-of-thought prompting can be improved by taking the majority final answer over many sampled generations (Wang et al., 2022a)). For LaMDA, we report averaged results over five random seeds, where each seed had a different randomly shuffled order of exemplars. As LaMDA experiments did not show large variance among different seeds, to save compute we report results for a single exemplar order for all other models.

## **3.2 Results**

The strongest results of chain-of-thought prompting are summarized in Figure 4, with all experimental outputs for each model collection, model size, and benchmark shown in Table 2 in the Appendix. There are three key takeaways. First, Figure 4 shows that chain-of-thought prompting is an emergent ability of model scale (Wei et al., 2022b). That is, chain-of-thought prompting does not positively impact performance for small models, and only yields performance gains when used with models of _∼_ 100B parameters. We qualitatively found that models of smaller scale produced fluent but illogical chains of thought, leading to lower performance than standard prompting.

4

Second, chain-of-thought prompting has larger performance gains for more-complicated problems. For instance, for GSM8K (the dataset with the lowest baseline performance), performance more than doubled for the largest GPT and PaLM models. On the other hand, for SingleOp, the easiest subset of MAWPS which only requires a single step to solve, performance improvements were either negative or very small (see Appendix Table 3).

Third, chain-of-thought prompting via GPT-3 175B and PaLM 540B compares favorably to prior state of the art, which typically finetunes a task-specific model on a labeled training dataset. Figure 4 shows how PaLM 540B uses chain-ofthought prompting to achieve new state of the art on GSM8K, SVAMP, and MAWPS (though note that standard prompting already passed the prior best for SVAMP). On the other two datasets, AQuA and ASDiv, PaLM with chain-of-thought prompting reaches within 2% of the state of the art (Appendix Table 2).

To better understand why chain-of-thought prompting works, we manually examined modelgenerated chains of thought by LaMDA 137B for GSM8K. Of 50 random examples where the model returned the correct final answer, all of the generated chains of thought were also logically and mathematically correct except two that coincidentally arrived at the correct answer (see Appendix D.1, and Table 8 for examples of correct model-generated chains of thought). We also randomly examined 50 random samples for which the model gave the wrong answer. The summary of this analysis is that 46% of the chains of thought were almost correct, barring minor mistakes (calculator error, symbol map-

To provide a small insight into why scaling improves chain-of-thought reasoning ability, we performed a similar analysis of errors made by PaLM 62B and whether those errors were fixed by scaling to PaLM 540B. The summary is that scaling PaLM to 540B fixes a large portion of one-step missing and semantic understanding errors in the 62B model (see Appendix A.1).

## **3.3 Ablation Study**

The observed benefits of using chain-of-thought prompting raises the natural question of whether the same performance improvements can be conferred via other types of prompting. Figure 5 shows an ablation study with three variations of chain of thought described below.

**Equation only.** One reason for why chain-of-thought prompting might help is that it produces the mathematical equation to be evaluated, and so we test a variation where the model is prompted to output only a mathematical equation before giving the answer. Figure 5 shows that equation only prompting does not help much for GSM8K, which implies that the semantics of the questions in GSM8K are too challenging to directly translate into an equation without the natural language reasoning steps in chain of thought. For datasets of one-step or two-step problems, however, we find that equation only prompting does improve performance, since the equation can be easily derived from the question (see Appendix Table 6).

5

**Variable compute only.** Another intuition is that chain of thought allows the model to spend more computation (i.e., intermediate tokens) on harder problems. To isolate the effect of variable computation from chain-of-thought reasoning, we test a configuration where the model is prompted to output a only sequence of dots ( _. . ._ ) equal to the number of characters in the equation needed to solve the problem. This variant performs about the same as the baseline, which suggests that variable computation by itself is not the reason for the success of chainof-thought prompting, and that there appears to be utility from expressing intermediate steps via natural language.

**Chain of thought after answer.** Another potential benefit of chain-of-thought prompting could simply be that such prompts allow the model to better access relevant knowledge acquired during pretraining. Therefore, we test an alternative configuration where the chain of thought prompt is only given after the answer, isolating whether the model actually depends on the produced chain of thought to give the final answer. This variant performs about the same as the baseline, which suggests that the sequential reasoning embodied in the chain of thought is useful for reasons beyond just activating knowledge.

## **3.4 Robustness of Chain of Thought**

Sensitivity to exemplars is a key consideration of prompting approaches—for instance, varying the permutation of few-shot exemplars can cause the accuracy of GPT-3 on SST-2 to range from near chance (54.3%) to near state of the art (93.4%) (Zhao et al., 2021). In this final subsection, we evaluate robustness to chains of thought written by different annotators. In addition to the results above, which used chains of thought written by an Annotator A, two other co-authors of this paper (Annotators B and C) independently wrote chains of thought for the same few-shot exemplars (shown in Appendix H). Annotator A also wrote another chain of thought that was more concise than the original, following the style of solutions given in Cobbe et al. (2021).[1]

Figure 6 shows these results for LaMDA 137B on GSM8K and MAWPS (ablation results for other datasets are given in Appendix Table 6 / Table 7). Although there is variance among different chain of thought annotations, as would be expected when using exemplar-based prompting (Le Scao and Rush, 2021; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021; Zhao et al., 2021), all sets of chain of thought prompts outperform the standard baseline by a large margin. This result implies that successful use of chain of thought does not depend on a particular linguistic style.

In addition to robustness to annotators, independently-written chains of thought, different exemplars, and various language models, we also find that chain-of-thought prompting for arithmetic reasoning is robust to different exemplar orders and varying numbers of exemplars (see Appendix A.2).

## **4 Commonsense Reasoning**

Although chain of thought is particularly suitable for math word problems, the language-based nature of chain of thought actually makes it applicable to a broad class of commonsense reasoning problems, which involve reasoning about physical and human interactions under the presumption of general background knowledge. Commonsense reasoning is key for interacting with the world and is still beyond the reach of current natural language understanding systems (Talmor et al., 2021).

**Benchmarks.** We consider five datasets covering a diverse range of commonsense reasoning types. The popular **CSQA** (Talmor et al., 2019) asks commonsense questions about the world involving complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. **StrategyQA** (Geva et al., 2021) requires models to infer a multi-hop strategy to answer questions. We choose two specialized evaluation sets from the BIG-bench effort (BIG-bench collaboration, 2021): **Date** Understanding, which involves inferring a date from a given context, and **Sports** Understanding, which involves determining whether a sentence relating to sports is plausible or implausible. Finally, the **SayCan** dataset (Ahn et al., 2022) involves mapping a natural language instruction to a sequence of robot actions from a discrete set. Figure 3 shows examples with chain of thought annotations for all datasets.

**Prompts.** We follow the same experimental setup as the prior section. For CSQA and StrategyQA, we randomly selected examples from the training set and manually composed chains of thought for them to use as few-shot exemplars. The two BIG-bench tasks do not have training sets, so we selected the first ten examples as exemplars in the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars and report numbers on the rest of the evaluation set. For SayCan, we use six examples from the training set used in Ahn et al. (2022) and also manually composed chains of thought.

**Results.** Figure 7 highlights these results for PaLM (full results for LaMDA, GPT-3, and different model scales are shown in Table 4). For all tasks, scaling up model size improved the performance of standard prompting; chain-of-thought prompting led to further gains, with improvements appearing to be largest for PaLM 540B. With chain-of-thought prompting, PaLM 540B achieved strong performance relative to baselines, outperforming the prior state of the art on StrategyQA (75.6% vs 69.4%) and outperforming an unaided sports enthusiast on sports understanding (95.4% vs 84%). These results demonstrate that chain-of-thought prompting can also improve performance on tasks requiring a range of commonsense reasoning abilities (though note that gain was minimal on CSQA).

## **5 Symbolic Reasoning**

Our final experimental evaluation considers symbolic reasoning, which is simple for humans but potentially challenging for language models. We show that chain-ofthought prompting not only enables language models to perform symbolic reasoning tasks that are challenging in the standard prompting setting, but also facilitates length generalization to inference-time inputs longer than those seen in the few-shot exemplars.

**Tasks.** We use the following two toy tasks.

- **Last letter concatenation.** This task asks the model to concatenate the last letters of words in a name (e.g.,

- _“Amy Brown” → “yn”_ ). It is a more challenging version of first letter concatenation, which language models can already perform without chain of thought.[3] We generate full names by randomly concatenating names from the top one-thousand first and last names from name census data ( `https://namecensus.com/` ).

- **Coin flip.** This task asks the model to answer whether a coin is still heads up after people either flip or don’t flip the coin (e.g., _“A coin is heads up. Phoebe flips the coin. Osvaldo does not flip the coin. Is the coin still heads up?” → “no”_ ).
As the construction of these symbolic reasoning tasks is well-defined, for each task we consider an _in-domain_ test set for which examples had the same number of steps as

For last letter concatenation, the model only sees exemplars of names with two words, and then performs last letter concatenation on names with 3 and 4 words.[4] We do the same for the number of potential flips in the coin flip task. Our experimental setup uses the same methods and models as in the prior two sections. We again manually compose chains of thought for the few-shot exemplars for each task, which are given in Figure 3.

**Results.** The results of these in-domain and OOD evaluations are shown in Figure 8 for PaLM, with results for LaMDA shown in Appendix Table 5. With PaLM 540B, chain-of-thought prompting leads to almost 100% solve rates (note that standard prompting already solves coin flip with PaLM 540, though not for LaMDA 137B). Note that these in-domain evaluations are “toy tasks” in the sense that perfect solution structures are already provided by the chains of thought in the few-shot exemplars; all the model has to do is repeat the same steps with the new symbols in the test-time example. And yet, small models still fail—the ability to perform abstract manipulations on unseen symbols for these three tasks only arises at the scale of 100B model parameters.

As for the OOD evaluations, standard prompting fails for both tasks. With chain-of-thought prompting, language models achieve upward scaling curves (though performance is lower than in the in-domain setting). Hence, chain-of-thought prompting facilitates length generalization beyond seen chains of thought for language models of sufficient scale.

## **6 Discussion**

We have explored chain-of-thought prompting as a simple mechanism for eliciting multi-step reasoning behavior in large language models. We first saw that chain-of-thought prompting improves performance by a large margin on arithmetic reasoning, yielding improvements that are much stronger than ablations and robust to different annotators, exemplars, and language models (Section 3). Next,

> 3We tested 10 common names using GPT-3 `davinci` and it got all but one correct.

> 4For names of length longer than 2 words, we concatenate multiple first and last names together.

8

experiments on commonsense reasoning underscored how the linguistic nature of chain-of-thought reasoning makes it generally applicable (Section 4). Finally, we showed that for symbolic reasoning, chain-of-thought prompting facilitates OOD generalization to longer sequence lengths (Section 5). In all experiments, chain-of-thought reasoning is elicited simply by prompting an off-the-shelf language model. No language models were finetuned in the process of writing this paper.

The emergence of chain-of-thought reasoning as a result of model scale has been a prevailing theme (Wei et al., 2022b). For many reasoning tasks where standard prompting has a flat scaling curve, chainof-thought prompting leads to dramatically increasing scaling curves. Chain-of-thought prompting appears to expand the set of tasks that large language models can perform successfully—in other words, our work underscores that standard prompting only provides a lower bound on the capabilities of large language models. This observation likely raises more questions than it answers—for instance, how much more can we expect reasoning ability to improve with a further increase in model scale? What other prompting methods might expand the range of tasks that language models can solve?

As for limitations, we first qualify that although chain of thought emulates the thought processes of human reasoners, this does not answer whether the neural network is actually “reasoning,” which we leave as an open question. Second, although the cost of manually augmenting exemplars with chains of thought is minimal in the few-shot setting, such annotation costs could be prohibitive for finetuning (though this could potentially be surmounted with synthetic data generation, or zero-shot generalization). Third, there is no guarantee of correct reasoning paths, which can lead to both correct and incorrect answers; improving factual generations of language models is an open direction for future work (Rashkin et al., 2021; Ye and Durrett, 2022; Wiegreffe et al., 2022, _inter alia_ ). Finally, the emergence of chain-of-thought reasoning only at large model scales makes it costly to serve in real-world applications; further research could explore how to induce reasoning in smaller models.

## **7 Related Work**

This work is inspired by many research areas, which we detail in an extended related work section (Appendix C). Here we describe two directions and associated papers that are perhaps most relevant.

The first relevant direction is using intermediate steps to solve reasoning problems. Ling et al. (2017) pioneer the idea of using natural language rationales to solve math word problems through a series of intermediate steps. Their work is a remarkable contrast to the literature using formal languages to reason (Roy et al., 2015; Chiang and Chen, 2019; Amini et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2019). Cobbe et al. (2021) extend Ling et al. (2017) by creating a larger dataset and using it to finetune a pretrained language model rather than training a model from scratch. In the domain of program synthesis, Nye et al. (2021) leverage language models to predict the final outputs of Python programs via first line-to-line predicting the intermediate computational results, and show that their step-by-step prediction method performs better than directly predicting the final outputs.

Naturally, this paper also relates closely to the large body of recent work on prompting. Since the popularization of few-shot prompting as given by Brown et al. (2020), several general approaches have improved the prompting ability of models, such as automatically learning prompts (Lester et al., 2021) or giving models instructions describing a task (Wei et al., 2022a; Sanh et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022). Whereas these approaches improve or augment the input part of the prompt (e.g., instructions that are prepended to inputs), our work takes the orthogonal direction of augmenting the outputs of language models with a chain of thought.

## **8 Conclusions**

We have explored chain-of-thought prompting as a simple and broadly applicable method for enhancing reasoning in language models. Through experiments on arithmetic, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning, we find that chain-of-thought reasoning is an emergent property of model scale that allows sufficiently large language models to perform reasoning tasks that otherwise have flat scaling curves. Broadening the range of reasoning tasks that language models can perform will hopefully inspire further work on language-based approaches to reasoning.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting as a simple yet powerful method to elicit multi-step reasoning in large language models, demonstrating substantial performance gains across arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. The work is well-motivated, clearly presented, and empirically rigorous, with ablation studies, robustness checks, and analysis across multiple model scales and tasks. It identifies a key emergent property of large models—few-shot reasoning via natural language decomposition—and has had significant influence on subsequent research in language model reasoning.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient evidence that performance gains stem from 'reasoning' rather than surface-level pattern matching: The paper attributes performance improvements to the elicitation of 'reasoning' abilities in language models, but does not provide sufficient evidence to distinguish genuine logical reasoning from sophisticated pattern matching on syntactic structures in the exemplars. For instance, the model may learn to replicate the form of chain-of-thought outputs—such as decomposing problems into sequential steps—without engaging in meaningful inference. The manual error analysis in Appendix D.1 notes that incorrect chains often contain plausible but flawed logic, yet the paper does not systematically test whether the model's final answer depends on the coherence of the generated steps. A stronger test would involve perturbing correct chains with invalid reasoning and measuring downstream impact on accuracy, or using consistency checks across multiple chains. Without such controls, the claim that CoT 'elicits reasoning' remains interpretive rather than demonstrated.

- Overreliance on manually crafted exemplars without analysis of generalization to diverse reasoning styles: The chain-of-thought prompts across all experiments rely on a small set of manually written exemplars, which raises concerns about potential overfitting to specific phrasing, structure, or annotation style. While Figure 6 shows results with different annotators, the variations are limited in scope and still reflect expert-designed reasoning paths. The paper does not explore how CoT performs when exemplars are generated by non-experts, contain suboptimal reasoning, or follow alternative decomposition strategies. This limits the claim of broad applicability: if performance depends on high-quality, human-crafted reasoning traces, then CoT may not generalize well to new domains without similar curation. Including an analysis of automatically generated or crowd-sourced chains would strengthen the method's practicality and robustness claims.

- Lack of out-of-distribution evaluation for arithmetic and commonsense tasks: While the symbolic reasoning section includes out-of-domain (OOD) generalization tests—e.g., longer names or more coin flips—the same is not done for arithmetic or commonsense benchmarks. The GSM8K and other math datasets use exemplars and test instances of similar complexity, leaving open whether CoT enables true compositional generalization beyond the demonstrated step count. Given that models can simply mimic the depth of reasoning in the few-shot examples, the impressive results may reflect in-distribution matching rather than scalable reasoning. Extending OOD evaluation to arithmetic problems with more steps than seen in prompts would better support the claim that CoT facilitates systematic generalization.

- Inadequate discussion of failure modes and limitations in real-world applicability: The paper acknowledges that CoT does not guarantee correct reasoning paths but does not deeply analyze when or why it fails, especially in high-stakes contexts. For example, the 54% of incorrect chains involving 'major errors in semantic understanding' (Section 3.2) suggest that models often hallucinate premises or misinterpret problem structure. The discussion would benefit from concrete examples of such failures and their implications for trustworthiness in deployment. Moreover, the computational cost of generating long reasoning traces—especially when combined with methods like self-consistency—is not addressed, despite its relevance for latency-sensitive applications. A more critical treatment of these limitations would improve the paper's balance and utility.

- Overstatement of 'emergent ability' without clarifying scale thresholds or causal mechanisms: The paper repeatedly refers to chain-of-thought reasoning as an 'emergent ability' of model scale, citing dramatic improvements only in models above 100B parameters. However, it does not define what constitutes emergence—whether it's a discontinuous jump in performance or a steep but smooth scaling trend—and does not analyze the exact parameter threshold at which CoT becomes effective. Without plotting fine-grained scaling curves across model sizes or isolating architectural factors (e.g., training data, tokenizer effects), the claim risks being tautological: 'CoT works because large models can do CoT.' A more precise characterization of the interaction between scale, data, and reasoning success would strengthen the scientific contribution.

- No out-of-distribution test for arithmetic reasoning generalization: The paper demonstrates strong performance on GSM8K and other arithmetic benchmarks but only evaluates in-distribution. While symbolic reasoning tasks include out-of-domain (OOD) tests—longer sequences than seen in exemplars—the same is not done for arithmetic problems. This leaves open whether chain-of-thought prompting enables true compositional generalization or simply improves matching within the demonstrated complexity range. Readers will expect evidence that the method generalizes beyond the reasoning depth seen in few-shot examples, especially given the claim that it supports systematic reasoning. To close this gap, the authors should construct an OOD arithmetic benchmark: for example, generate GSM8K-style problems requiring more steps (e.g., 5–6 operations) than any in the few-shot exemplars (which are mostly 2–3 steps), and test whether performance still scales with model size under chain-of-thought prompting.

- Missing analysis of when chain-of-thought fails for commonsense tasks: The paper shows gains on commonsense reasoning tasks like StrategyQA and Date Understanding but provides little insight into failure modes, especially for questions where the chain of thought leads astray. A natural question is whether incorrect chains stem from flawed factual premises, invalid inference, or misparsing the question. Without such analysis, the claim of improved reasoning remains superficial. For instance, the paper could examine 50 failed cases on StrategyQA, categorize errors (e.g., wrong assumption, broken logic, hallucinated knowledge), and assess whether larger models reduce specific error types. This would strengthen the discussion of robustness and help distinguish reasoning improvements from mere answer accuracy.

- No comparison to alternative prompting strategies on symbolic tasks: For symbolic reasoning, the paper shows chain-of-thought prompting enables length generalization but does not compare to simpler baselines that might achieve similar gains. For example, could a model prompted only to output intermediate states (e.g., 'Heads → Tails → Tails') without natural language reasoning replicate the result? Or would a template-based decomposition strategy suffice? Since the tasks are algorithmic, the improvement might reflect structured output formatting rather than reasoning per se. To validate the necessity of natural language chains, the authors should ablate against a 'symbolic step-only' prompt variant—e.g., listing transformations without explanation—and show that only full chain-of-thought prompting enables generalization.

- Lack of guidance on prompt construction for new tasks: The paper presents chain-of-thought prompting as broadly applicable but offers no practical guidance on how to design effective exemplars for unseen domains. Practitioners will struggle to replicate the method without knowing what properties make a good chain of thought: level of detail, choice of intermediate steps, or phrasing style. The robustness analysis in Figure 6 varies annotators but doesn’t isolate which features matter. The authors should add a short section or appendix with concrete principles—e.g., 'include all arithmetic operations explicitly' or 'state assumptions before reasoning'—derived from their best-performing prompts, possibly validated through ablation on a held-out task.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide stronger evidence distinguishing genuine reasoning from pattern matching, such as through perturbation analysis or consistency checks on generated chains.

- Expand robustness evaluation to include diverse, non-expert-generated chain-of-thought exemplars to assess generalization across reasoning styles.

- Add out-of-distribution evaluations for arithmetic and commonsense tasks, testing generalization to problems with more reasoning steps than seen in prompts.

- Include a detailed analysis of failure modes with representative examples and discuss computational costs and real-world feasibility.

- Clarify the definition and evidence for 'emergent ability' by analyzing performance trends across model scales and identifying thresholds or contributing factors.

comments.title: Overstated claim of emergent reasoning without evidence of discontinuity
comments.quote: Through experiments on arithmetic, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning, we find that chain-of-thought reasoning is an emergent property of model scale that allows sufficiently large language models to perform reasoning tasks that otherwise have flat scaling curves.
comments.explanation: The term 'emergent property' implies a qualitative shift or discontinuous jump in behavior at a threshold scale, but the paper presents only continuous, steep scaling curves without evidence of a phase transition. No fine-grained analysis across model sizes is provided to distinguish smooth improvement from true emergence. To avoid overstatement, rephrase to reflect the observed correlation: 'chain-of-thought prompting enables larger models to solve tasks with limited gains under standard prompting, indicating a strong dependence on scale.'

comments.title: Inconsistent model nomenclature undermines clarity
comments.quote: With PaLM 540B, chain-of-thought prompting leads to almost  $100\%$  solve rates (note that standard prompting already solves coin flip with PaLM 540, though not for LaMDA 137B).
comments.explanation: The model 'PaLM 540' is used without the 'B' suffix, while all other references use 'PaLM 540B' for the 540-billion-parameter model. This inconsistency introduces ambiguity—readers may interpret 'PaLM 540' as a smaller variant. Since no such model is defined or used elsewhere, this appears to be a typographical error. Rename 'PaLM 540' to 'PaLM 540B' for terminological consistency.

comments.title: Claim of 'state-of-the-art' lacks temporal and comparative grounding
comments.quote: prompting a PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier.
comments.explanation: The term 'state-of-the-art' is presented as absolute but is time- and context-dependent. The paper does not cite a specific leaderboard or date-stamped benchmark to confirm this status. While the comparison to Cobbe et al. (2021) is valid, other models like Codex may have achieved comparable results by 2022. To maintain scientific rigor, qualify the claim: 'achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among published results at the time' or cite a specific source like the GSM8K leaderboard as of submission.

comments.title: Failure to define chain-of-thought structure for non-math tasks
comments.quote: The language-based nature of chain of thought actually makes it applicable to a broad class of commonsense reasoning problems, which involve reasoning about physical and human interactions under the presumption of general background knowledge.
comments.explanation: While the paper extends chain-of-thought prompting to commonsense tasks, it does not define what constitutes a 'chain of thought' in these domains. For arithmetic problems, intermediate steps are concrete (e.g., equations), but for tasks like Sports Understanding or StrategyQA, the form of reasoning—logical inference, narrative justification, or knowledge assertion—is left unspecified. Readers cannot assess whether the same mechanism applies. Add an example or brief definition of a chain of thought for a non-math task to clarify continuity.

comments.title: Missing control for linguistic complexity in ablation study
comments.quote: Figure 5 shows an ablation study with three variations of chain of thought described below.
comments.explanation: The ablation study lacks a control that matches the chain-of-thought prompt in length and linguistic complexity but lacks logical structure. Without such a condition—e.g., a grammatical but irrelevant passage—the study cannot rule out that gains arise from increased language exposure rather than reasoning content. The 'variable compute only' condition uses dots, which are not linguistically comparable. Add a control with semantically unrelated natural language to isolate the effect of coherent reasoning.

comments.title: Invalid control in 'variable compute only' ablation
comments.quote: To isolate the effect of variable computation from chain-of-thought reasoning, we test a configuration where the model is prompted to output a only sequence of dots (... ) equal to the number of characters in the equation needed to solve the problem.
comments.explanation: The 'variable compute only' condition requires the model to output a number of dots equal to the character length of the equation—information that is only known after solving the problem. This creates a logical inconsistency: the model must implicitly solve the task to generate the correct number of tokens. A valid test would use fixed-length irrelevant tokens (e.g., 50 random words) to isolate computation time. Otherwise, the control fails to disentangle computation from reasoning.

comments.title: Overgeneralization from expert annotators to arbitrary linguistic styles
comments.quote: This result implies that successful use of chain of thought does not depend on a particular linguistic style.
comments.explanation: The conclusion is drawn from comparisons among three expert co-authors, all familiar with formal reasoning conventions. Their chains likely share structural similarities—stepwise decomposition, imperative phrasing—despite surface variation. There is no evidence of robustness to non-expert, informal, or divergent styles. The claim overreaches: it should be limited to 'chain-of-thought prompting is robust across expert-generated variations in reasoning style.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of symbolic abstraction over unseen symbols
comments.quote: And yet, small models still fail—the ability to perform abstract manipulations on unseen symbols for these three tasks only arises at the scale of 100B model parameters.
comments.explanation: The claim assumes test-time names (e.g., 'Phoebe', 'Osvaldo') are truly unseen, but the paper does not confirm that these names were excluded from pretraining data. If the model has encountered them before, performance may reflect pattern matching rather than symbolic abstraction. To support the claim, either verify symbol novelty or use artificial names (e.g., 'Xel', 'Trok') in evaluation. Otherwise, remove the assertion about 'unseen symbols.'

comments.title: Misleading human performance comparison without methodology
comments.quote: With chain-of-thought prompting, PaLM 540B achieved strong performance relative to baselines, outperforming the prior state of the art on StrategyQA (75.6% vs 69.4%) and outperforming an unaided sports enthusiast on sports understanding (95.4% vs 84%).
comments.explanation: The 84% human baseline is unexplained—no details are given on how it was measured, who the 'enthusiasts' were, or whether the evaluation was controlled. Without citation or methodology, this comparison risks being misleading. Either cite a source for the human performance or clarify that the figure is an informal estimate not from a structured study.

comments.title: Lack of ablation for reasoning content vs. added detail
comments.quote: For CSQA and StrategyQA, we randomly selected examples from the training set and manually composed chains of thought for them to use as few-shot exemplars.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes gains to reasoning structure but does not control for the added specificity or detail in the exemplars. A critical ablation would compare chain-of-thought prompts to 'direct answer' prompts with equivalent detail but no intermediate steps. Without this, it remains unclear whether performance stems from reasoning or simply from richer context. Add such a control to isolate the contribution of step-by-step decomposition.

comments.title: Missing OOD performance quantification
comments.quote: With chain-of-thought prompting, language models achieve upward scaling curves (though performance is lower than in the in-domain setting).
comments.explanation: The paper mentions OOD performance is 'lower' and shows 'upward scaling curves' but does not report actual accuracy values in the main text. Figure 8 is referenced but not included in the provided content, and Appendix Table 5 is not discussed. Without concrete numbers, the claim of successful length generalization remains qualitative. Report OOD accuracy for both tasks and models in the main text or ensure the figure is properly included.

comments.title: Data leakage in BIG-bench evaluation setup
comments.quote: The two BIG-bench tasks do not have training sets, so we selected the first ten examples as exemplars in the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars and report numbers on the rest of the evaluation set.
comments.explanation: Using examples from the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars violates standard evaluation protocols. This introduces data leakage and risks inflating performance, especially if the first ten examples are atypical or easier. Few-shot exemplars should come from a separate development set or be explicitly acknowledged as part of a leave-k-out setup. Otherwise, the integrity of the evaluation is compromised.

comments.title: Overstated necessity of chain-of-thought for symbolic tasks
comments.quote: With PaLM 540B, chain-of-thought prompting leads to almost  $100\%$  solve rates (note that standard prompting already solves coin flip with PaLM 540, though not for LaMDA 137B).
comments.explanation: The parenthetical note states that standard prompting already solves the coin flip task with PaLM 540B, which undermines the claim that chain-of-thought is necessary for success. The key contribution is length generalization, not in-domain performance. Clarify that standard prompting works for in-domain coin flip but fails on out-of-domain versions, where chain-of-thought uniquely enables generalization.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, a method where few-shot exemplars include intermediate reasoning steps. The authors demonstrate that this technique significantly improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks for models with over 100B parameters, often reaching or exceeding state-of-the-art results from fine-tuned models.

The paper identifies a major emergent property of large language models and provides a simple, highly effective method for unlocking multi-step reasoning without fine-tuning. Its significance lies in shifting the focus from model scaling alone to the importance of how models are prompted and the structured data they process at inference time.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity between logical reasoning and pattern matching: In Section 6, the authors acknowledge that it remains an open question whether the model is actually 'reasoning' or just emulating a thought process. However, the paper often uses the term 'reasoning' as an established fact. The analysis in Section 3.2 shows that 2 out of 50 correct answers were 'coincidental,' which suggests the model might be mapping input patterns to output patterns without internalizing the logic. To strengthen the claim that this is an elicitation of reasoning, the authors should include a 'shuffled' or 'random' chain-of-thought ablation. If providing a logically incoherent but structurally similar chain still improves performance, it would suggest the model is benefiting from the formatting or the 'time to think' rather than the logical sequence itself.

- Lack of verification for intermediate step validity: The primary metric used throughout Sections 3, 4, and 5 is final answer accuracy. While Section 3.2 provides a manual error analysis for a small sample (50 cases), the paper lacks a systematic metric to evaluate the logical validity of the intermediate steps across the entire benchmark. A model could arrive at the correct answer through flawed logic, which would be a 'false positive' for reasoning. The authors should implement a more rigorous evaluation, perhaps using a subset of the data where intermediate steps are verified by a separate model or human annotators, to ensure that the performance gains are not inflated by 'right for the wrong reasons' scenarios.

- Potential data contamination in pre-training corpora: The benchmarks used, such as GSM8K and CSQA (Sections 3.1 and 4), are popular and have been publicly available for years. Given that models like PaLM and GPT-3 are trained on massive web crawls, there is a high risk that these specific reasoning problems or their variants appeared in the training data. This would mean the model is performing a retrieval or memorization task rather than zero-shot reasoning. The authors should address this by testing on a small, entirely new 'hidden' set of problems created after the model's training cutoff or by performing a n-gram overlap analysis between the benchmarks and the training sets.

- Incomplete exploration of sensitivity to prompt phrasing: Section 3.4 discusses robustness across three annotators, but the scope of this analysis is narrow. Prompting is notoriously sensitive to specific keywords, punctuation, and the ordering of examples. While the authors show that CoT generally outperforms standard prompting, they do not provide a systematic sensitivity analysis for the 'style' of the chain. For instance, does using 'Step 1, Step 2' vs. 'First, Then' significantly alter the results? A more detailed ablation study on the linguistic features of the chain of thought would clarify if the benefit comes from the specific natural language used or the mere presence of intermediate tokens.

- Absence of comparison with specialized symbolic solvers: In Section 5, the authors test symbolic tasks like 'last letter concatenation.' While they compare CoT to standard prompting, they do not contextualize these results against specialized symbolic or algorithmic solvers. While the paper's goal is to show general-purpose LLM capabilities, the claim that LLMs can now solve these tasks is less meaningful without acknowledging that these are 'toy' versions of problems that simpler, non-LLM systems solve with 100% accuracy. Adding a brief discussion or comparison regarding the efficiency and reliability of CoT versus symbolic AI for these specific tasks would provide a more balanced view of the method's utility.

- Missing analysis of failure modes in the emergent scale regime: The paper identifies chain-of-thought (CoT) as an emergent property of models with 100B+ parameters, yet it provides little detail on why the 540B model still fails on nearly 40% of GSM8K problems. While Section 3.2 mentions that 54% of errors are 'major,' it does not categorize these by reasoning type—such as arithmetic versus logical branching errors. To make this result bite, the authors should provide a breakdown of whether failures at the 540B scale are due to 'hallucinated' facts or a breakdown in the transitive property of the chain. Specifically, computing the error distribution across the different categories mentioned in the 62B vs 540B comparison would clarify if scaling merely reduces noise or actually solves specific classes of logical fallacies.

- Underdeveloped discussion on the necessity of natural language for symbolic tasks: The paper claims in Section 5 that CoT facilitates length generalization for symbolic tasks like 'last letter concatenation,' but it does not test if the natural language component is actually required for this generalization. Since these tasks are purely algorithmic, it is unclear if the model benefits from the linguistic 'reasoning' or simply the increased sequence length (token budget). The authors should include a 'code-like' or 'symbolic-only' intermediate step ablation for the coin flip task—for example, using a sequence of state transitions like 'H -> flip -> T -> no flip -> T' instead of natural language sentences. This would determine if the 'natural language' aspect of the chain of thought is a requirement for symbolic reasoning or just one possible format for state tracking.

- Missing implementation guidance for automated chain-of-thought generation: The paper positions CoT as a more efficient alternative to rationale-augmented finetuning because it only requires a few-shot prompt. However, the paper relies entirely on manually written chains of thought, which limits the method's practical scalability for new, complex domains not covered in the paper. A major gap is the lack of discussion on how a practitioner should construct these chains for a novel task to ensure model adherence. To address this, the authors should provide a 'recipe' or a set of principles used by Annotators A, B, and C—such as the optimal number of steps per chain or the level of granularity required—and test if a 'minimalist' versus 'maximalist' chain length significantly impacts the final answer accuracy.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include an ablation study using 'incorrect' or 'shuffled' chains of thought to isolate the effect of logical coherence from structural formatting.

- Perform a data contamination check by testing on a small set of novel, manually created reasoning problems not present in public datasets.

- Add a systematic evaluation of intermediate step accuracy for at least one benchmark to quantify 'right for the wrong reasons' occurrences.

- Expand the sensitivity analysis to include more varied linguistic styles and prompt structures beyond the three annotators currently listed.

comments.title: Flawed Isolation of Variable Computation
comments.quote: To isolate the effect of variable computation from chain-of-thought reasoning, we test a configuration where the model is prompted to output a only sequence of dots (... ) equal to the number of characters in the equation needed to solve the problem.
comments.explanation: This ablation fails to isolate 'variable computation' because it replaces semantic tokens with non-semantic filler tokens (dots). In transformer architectures, the attention mechanism relies on the content of previous tokens to compute the next; a sequence of dots provides no state-tracking or scratchpad utility, whereas a chain of thought provides a meaningful history for the model to attend to. This experiment only proves that 'filler tokens' do not help, not that the increased token budget itself is irrelevant if used for state-tracking. To properly isolate the effect of computation time, the authors should have used a sequence of tokens that allow for state-tracking without natural language, such as a sequence of random but distinct symbols. The claim should acknowledge that the 'dots' ablation specifically tests the lack of semantic content rather than just the computational budget.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding computational allocation
comments.quote: First, chain of thought, in principle, allows models to decompose multi-step problems into intermediate steps, which means that additional computation can be allocated to problems that require more reasoning steps.
comments.explanation: The claim that CoT allows for 'additional computation' is technically imprecise in the context of standard Transformer architectures. In a Transformer, the amount of computation (FLOPs) per token is constant, determined by the model's depth and width. While generating more tokens does increase the total FLOPs for the entire sequence, it does not change the computational density or the 'thinking time' per logic step. A problem requiring 10 steps of reasoning gets more total tokens, but each individual step is still constrained by the same single-pass transformation as a simple task. The authors should specify that CoT increases the total sequence-level computation rather than implying a dynamic allocation of processing power per reasoning unit.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in 'Chain of Thought after Answer' Ablation
comments.quote: Therefore, we test an alternative configuration where the chain of thought prompt is only given after the answer, isolating whether the model actually depends on the produced chain of thought to give the final answer. This variant performs about the same as the baseline, which suggests that the sequential reasoning embodied in the chain of thought is useful for reasons beyond just activating knowledge.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that the model 'depends' on the chain of thought is logically sound, but the interpretation that this 'isolates' knowledge activation is flawed. If the model provides the answer first, it is by definition performing standard prompting for the answer generation; the subsequent chain of thought is a post-hoc justification that cannot influence the already-generated answer. The fact that it performs 'about the same as the baseline' is the expected result of any post-answer generation. This does not prove that the chain of thought 'activates knowledge' in a way that standard prompting doesn't; it only confirms that the model cannot use information generated after the answer to change that answer.

comments.title: Inconsistent baseline comparison for BIG-bench tasks
comments.quote: The two BIG-bench tasks do not have training sets, so we selected the first ten examples as exemplars in the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars and report numbers on the rest of the evaluation set.
comments.explanation: The authors use the first ten examples of the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars for BIG-bench tasks, but they do not specify if the 'standard prompting' baseline used these same ten examples or a different set. If the standard prompting baseline used a different selection of examples or a different number of shots, the comparison is not controlled. This is particularly problematic for small evaluation sets where the specific choice of few-shot examples can significantly swing accuracy. To ensure a fair comparison, the authors must use the exact same ten examples for both standard and chain-of-thought prompting.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'emerge naturally' claim
comments.quote: In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models via a simple method called chain-of-thought prompting, where a few chain of thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in prompting.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that reasoning abilities 'emerge naturally,' but the method described relies on human-engineered 'chain of thought demonstrations' provided as exemplars. This creates a contradiction: if the ability requires specific external prompting with human-written logic to be activated, it is an elicited capability rather than one that 'emerges naturally' in the way scaling laws usually describe unsupervised feature acquisition. The phrasing suggests the model would perform these steps spontaneously, which is not what the experiments demonstrate. Rewriting 'emerge naturally' to 'can be elicited' would more accurately reflect that the behavior is dependent on the few-shot prompt structure.

comments.title: Ambiguous error categorization in manual analysis
comments.quote:
the wrong answer. The summary of this analysis is that  $46\%$  of the chains of thought were almost correct, barring minor mistakes (calculator error, symbol map

ping error, or one reasoning step missing), and that the other  $54\%$  of the chains of thought had major errors in semantic understanding or coherence (see Appendix D.2).
comments.explanation: The classification of 'one reasoning step missing' as a 'minor mistake' in the 46% category is logically problematic for a reasoning task. In multi-step math problems, a missing reasoning step typically invalidates the logical chain, making it a structural failure rather than a minor calculation error. This categorization risks inflating the perceived 'near-correctness' of the model's logic. The sentence should move 'one reasoning step missing' into the 'major errors' category or provide a specific definition of how a chain can be 'almost correct' while missing a necessary logical link.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in the definition of symbolic reasoning difficulty
comments.quote: Note that these in-domain evaluations are "toy tasks" in the sense that perfect solution structures are already provided by the chains of thought in the few-shot exemplars; all the model has to do is repeat the same steps with the new symbols in the test-time example. And yet, small models still fail—the ability to perform abstract manipulations on unseen symbols for these three tasks only arises at the scale of 100B model parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors characterize the task as merely repeating 'the same steps with the new symbols,' which describes a simple template-matching or substitution process rather than 'abstract manipulation' or 'reasoning.' If the model is simply following a provided structural template, the failure of smaller models (under 100B) is more likely due to a lack of instruction-following or context-window coherence rather than a lack of symbolic reasoning capacity. The claim that this ability 'only arises' at 100B+ parameters is undermined by the admission that the solution structure is explicitly provided.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of prompt structure
comments.quote: ations. Specifically, we explore the ability of language models to perform few-shot prompting for reasoning tasks, given a prompt that consists of triples:  $\langle$  input, chain of thought,
comments.explanation: The text defines the prompt as a series of triples consisting of <input, chain of thought, output>. However, Figure 1 and the standard few-shot prompting paradigm used in this paper actually use a prompt structure where the final test instance is an incomplete triple (input only), and the model is expected to generate the chain of thought and output. Defining the prompt itself as a set of triples is technically inaccurate as it ignores the test query that triggers the generation. It would be more precise to state that the prompt contains exemplars formatted as triples followed by a target input.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting as a method to elicit reasoning in large language models. While the empirical results are impressive and the approach is innovative, there are significant concerns about the robustness of the claims, the completeness of the evaluation, and the framing of the contribution. The paper would benefit from more rigorous analysis of failure modes, better statistical validation, and clearer articulation of limitations.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating that CoT prompting can unlock reasoning abilities in large language models, particularly on arithmetic and symbolic tasks. The emergence phenomenon with model scale is striking and the state-of-the-art results on GSM8K are notable. However, the evaluation lacks statistical rigor, the analysis of failure modes is superficial, and the paper overstates the generalizability of the approach without sufficient evidence.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Statistical Validation: The paper reports impressive performance gains but provides no statistical significance testing or confidence intervals for the results. Given the known sensitivity of prompting methods to small changes (as acknowledged in Section 3.4), readers cannot determine whether the reported gains are robust or could be due to random variation. This is particularly important for claims about emergence phenomena, where small differences in performance could be misinterpreted as phase transitions. The paper should include multiple runs with different random seeds and report standard deviations or confidence intervals for all major results.

- Superficial Failure Mode Analysis: Section 3.2's manual analysis of 50 correct and 50 incorrect examples is insufficient for understanding CoT's limitations. The binary categorization of errors into 'minor mistakes' vs 'major errors' lacks granularity and provides no insight into systematic failure patterns. More importantly, the paper does not analyze whether CoT introduces new types of errors compared to standard prompting, nor does it examine error propagation through the reasoning chain. A comprehensive error taxonomy with quantitative analysis across the full evaluation set would strengthen the paper's claims about CoT's reliability.

- Inconsistent Emergence Claims Across Tasks: The paper strongly emphasizes that CoT reasoning is an emergent property of model scale, but the evidence is mixed across tasks. While arithmetic tasks show clear emergence (Figure 4), commonsense reasoning tasks like CSQA show minimal gains (Section 4). The paper does not adequately explain this discrepancy or provide criteria for when emergence occurs. This raises questions about whether the claimed emergence is task-dependent rather than a general property of scale. The authors should analyze emergence patterns across tasks and provide a more nuanced discussion of the conditions under which CoT benefits emerge.

- Limited Evaluation of CoT Quality: The paper evaluates CoT primarily through final answer accuracy, without systematically assessing the quality of the reasoning chains themselves. While Section 3.2 briefly mentions that correct answers usually correspond to correct reasoning, there is no evaluation of reasoning quality for cases where the final answer is wrong. This misses an opportunity to understand whether CoT provides interpretable reasoning even when it fails to reach the correct answer. The paper should include human evaluation of reasoning quality independent of answer correctness, particularly for commonsense tasks where reasoning chains are more subjective.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons: The paper compares CoT primarily against standard prompting, but lacks comparisons against other intermediate reasoning methods that could serve as stronger baselines. For example, the 'equation only' ablation (Section 3.3) is a strawman comparison; more meaningful baselines would include methods like program synthesis, tool-augmented reasoning, or structured prompting approaches. The claim that CoT achieves state-of-the-art on GSM8K is also weakened by not comparing against more recent verifier-augmented approaches beyond the cited GPT-3+verifier baseline.

- Overstated Generalizability Claims: The paper claims CoT is 'potentially applicable to any task that humans can solve via language' (Section 2), but only evaluates on a narrow set of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic tasks. The literature context notes that CoT can degrade performance on pattern-matching tasks, yet the paper does not acknowledge this limitation or attempt to characterize task types where CoT is harmful. The authors should temper their generalizability claims and include experiments on tasks where CoT is expected to underperform to provide a balanced view.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuousness: The paper shows that chain-of-thought prompting improves performance, but it doesn't demonstrate that the result has bite by showing where the method fails when the conditions aren't met. For a paper claiming emergence as a function of model scale, there should be a clear example showing that smaller models fail to benefit from chain-of-thought prompting on the same tasks. The authors should include a specific DGP or model scale threshold where chain-of-thought prompting provides no benefit or even hurts performance, demonstrating that the emergence claim is non-vacuous. This would involve showing flat scaling curves for models below 100B parameters on the GSM8K benchmark with chain-of-thought prompting.

- No worked parametric example: The paper lacks a concrete, fully-specified worked example showing how chain-of-thought prompting operates on a specific model architecture. While Figure 1 shows an example prompt, the paper doesn't walk through a complete computation for a standard model from the literature. The authors should select a specific transformer architecture (e.g., GPT-3 175B) and demonstrate step-by-step how the attention mechanisms process a chain-of-thought prompt, showing how intermediate tokens influence the final answer computation. This would help readers understand the mechanistic basis for why chain-of-thought works rather than treating it as a black box.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper claims practical relevance but provides minimal guidance for implementing chain-of-thought prompting in real applications. Beyond the eight exemplars shown in Appendix Table 20, there's no discussion of how to select or generate chain-of-thought examples for new tasks, no analysis of computational overhead, and no mention of inference latency implications. The authors should include a section on practical implementation considerations: how many exemplars are typically needed, strategies for creating effective chain-of-thought demonstrations, and computational cost analysis comparing standard vs chain-of-thought prompting across different model sizes.

- Missing formal comparison to finetuning baselines: The paper claims chain-of-thought prompting surpasses finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier on GSM8K but never formally shows that the finetuning baseline is recovered as a special case. For a paper positioning itself against training-based approaches, there should be a direct comparison showing when and how chain-of-thought prompting matches or exceeds the performance of finetuning on the same data. The authors should include experiments comparing chain-of-thought prompting against finetuning on the same set of exemplars used for prompting, demonstrating the trade-off between annotation cost and performance. This would strengthen the claim that prompting is a viable alternative to finetuning.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance testing with multiple runs and confidence intervals for all major results

- Conduct comprehensive failure mode analysis with quantitative error taxonomy across the full evaluation set

- Include stronger baseline comparisons beyond standard prompting, particularly program synthesis and tool-augmented methods

- Add evaluation of reasoning chain quality independent of final answer correctness through human assessment

- Provide nuanced discussion of emergence patterns and task-dependent effects, including experiments on tasks where CoT may underperform

comments.title: Variable compute ablation confounds reasoning
comments.quote: To isolate the effect of variable computation from chain-of-thought reasoning, we test a configuration where the model is prompted to output a only sequence of dots (... ) equal to the number of characters in the equation needed to solve the problem.
comments.explanation: The variable compute ablation method is fundamentally flawed because it requires the model to first determine the correct equation (which necessitates reasoning) before counting its characters to output the corresponding number of dots. This means the model still performs the core reasoning step, making it impossible to isolate computation from reasoning effects. The ablation fails to achieve its stated goal of separating variable computation from chain-of-thought reasoning. Rewrite the variable compute configuration to use a predetermined number of dots independent of the specific problem's solution, or use a baseline computation amount that doesn't require problem-specific reasoning.

comments.title: Chain of thought after answer tests wrong hypothesis
comments.quote: Therefore, we test an alternative configuration where the chain of thought prompt is only given after the answer, isolating whether the model actually depends on the produced chain of thought to give the final answer.
comments.explanation: The ablation design is logically inconsistent with its stated purpose. If the chain of thought is provided after the answer, the model cannot use it to derive the answer since the answer must come first. This configuration tests whether post-answer chain of thought affects performance, not whether the model depends on chain of thought to produce the answer. To properly test dependency on chain of thought, compare scenarios where chain of thought is provided before the answer versus not provided at all. Remove this ablation or redesign it to test the actual hypothesis about chain-of-thought dependency.

comments.title: Contradictory SOTA achievement claim
comments.quote: Figure 4 shows how PaLM 540B uses chain-of-thought prompting to achieve new state of the art on GSM8K, SVAMP, and MAWPS (though note that standard prompting already passed the prior best for SVAMP).
comments.explanation: This statement contains a logical contradiction. If standard prompting already passed the prior best for SVAMP, then chain-of-thought prompting cannot be achieving 'new state of the art' on SVAMP - it would at best be maintaining the already achieved SOTA. The parenthetical note undermines the main claim about achieving new SOTA on all three mentioned benchmarks. Rewrite the sentence to state that new SOTA was achieved on GSM8K and MAWPS, while noting that for SVAMP, both standard and chain-of-thought prompting exceeded prior SOTA.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance doubling claim
comments.quote: For instance, for GSM8K (the dataset with the lowest baseline performance), performance more than doubled for the largest GPT and PaLM models.
comments.explanation: The claim that performance 'more than doubled' for GSM8K lacks supporting numerical evidence in this section. While Figure 4 is referenced, the text does not provide the specific baseline and chain-of-thought performance numbers that would allow verification of this claim. Without concrete values showing the exact performance metrics, readers cannot confirm whether the improvement actually exceeded 100% relative gain. Rewrite this sentence to include the specific performance numbers or remove the 'more than doubled' claim because the evidence is not presented in this section.

comments.title: Missing exemplar selection methodology
comments.quote: As most of the datasets only have an evaluation split, we manually composed a set of eight few-shot exemplars with chains of thought for prompting—Figure 1 (right) shows one chain of thought exemplar, and the full set of exemplars is given in Appendix Table 20. (These particular exemplars did not undergo prompt engineering; robustness is studied in Section 3.4 and Appendix A.2.)
comments.explanation: The paper states that exemplars were 'manually composed' but provides no methodology for how these eight exemplars were selected or constructed. Without a clear selection criterion, the results may depend on the specific choice of exemplars rather than the chain-of-thought approach itself. The lack of selection methodology makes reproducibility difficult and raises questions about potential cherry-picking. Add a description of the exemplar selection process, including criteria for choosing specific problems and how chains of thought were constructed.

comments.title: Inconsistent exemplar selection across tasks
comments.quote: For CSQA and StrategyQA, we randomly selected examples from the training set and manually composed chains of thought for them to use as few-shot exemplars. The two BIG-bench tasks do not have training sets, so we selected the first ten examples as exemplars in the evaluation set as few-shot exemplars and report numbers on the rest of the evaluation set.
comments.explanation: The exemplar selection methodology is inconsistent across tasks, potentially biasing results. Random selection with manual chain composition for CSQA/StrategyQA differs from using the first ten examples for BIG-bench tasks. This systematic difference could affect performance comparisons. Clarify why different selection strategies were used or standardize the approach across all tasks because methodological consistency is essential for fair comparison.

comments.title: Unspecified threshold claim
comments.quote: And yet, small models still fail—the ability to perform abstract manipulations on unseen symbols for these three tasks only arises at the scale of 100B model parameters.
comments.explanation: The paper claims abilities 'only arises at the scale of 100B model parameters' but only provides results for PaLM 540B and LaMDA 137B. No evidence is shown for models around 100B parameters to establish this threshold. Add empirical evidence showing performance at/around 100B parameters or soften the claim to reflect the actual model sizes tested (e.g., 'arises at scales of 100B+ parameters based on the models evaluated').

comments.title: State-of-the-art claim lacks temporal context
comments.quote: For instance, prompting a PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims state-of-the-art performance on GSM8K but doesn't specify when this claim holds. Since this is a 2022 paper, the state-of-the-art claim is time-bound and may be outdated by newer methods. More importantly, the claim compares against 'finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier' but doesn't specify which verifier approach or provide the exact accuracy numbers. Without these details, readers cannot assess the magnitude of the claimed improvement. Rewrite the sentence to include specific accuracy numbers and clarify the temporal context: 'For instance, prompting a PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves X% accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark, surpassing the Y% accuracy of finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier [Cobbe et al., 2021].'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated sports enthusiast comparison
comments.quote: outperforming an unaided sports enthusiast on sports understanding (95.4% vs 84%).
comments.explanation: The comparison to an 'unaided sports enthusiast' lacks methodological justification.
